does: This paper identifies and addresses the reflection gap in LLM agents—their tendency to misassess their own performance even when receiving concrete environment feedback like execution results or error messages. The authors propose RefGRPO, which augments standard reinforcement learning with a calibration bonus computed by contrasting the agent's self-reflection against actual outcomes, along with a dynamically scheduled coefficient for this bonus. Importantly, this calibration signal requires no additional reward model, LLM judge, or external annotations. Evaluated on text-to-SQL tasks across multiple benchmarks, RefGRPO simultaneously reduces underconfidence rates from 44.4% to 7.7% and improves task accuracy from 75.1% to 76.5%, demonstrating that accurate self-assessment and task performance can be jointly optimized.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of LLM agents that learn from environmental feedback through reinforcement learning, extending prior research on agent calibration and credit assignment in RL. It responds to known challenges in agentic systems where agents fail to properly learn from execution feedback despite having access to it—a problem related to the broader challenge of ensuring agents can accurately model their own competence. The paper connects to work on RL training for language models, particularly group relative policy optimization and other methods for improving agent behavior through feedback. It also engages with the classical problem of calibration in machine learning, adapting these insights to the specific context of agents that must self-assess their reasoning and execution outcomes.
enables: This work opens directions for improving the general reliability and self-awareness of deployed LLM agents across diverse domains beyond text-to-SQL. By showing that self-reflection calibration and task performance improve together without requiring expensive external judgments, it suggests a scalable path toward agents that can better monitor their own failures and successes. The method potentially enables more robust agentic systems in complex environments where agents must reason about uncertainty and handle diverse tool outputs. Future work could explore whether similar calibration approaches apply to multimodal agents, longer-horizon planning tasks, or settings with partial observability, and whether improved self-reflection enables better error recovery and adaptive learning strategies in interactive environments.
